meta.book: Esther (Addition E: The King's Second Decree)

preamble.summary: Addition E, inserted after Esther 8:12, provides the full text of King Artaxerxes' second decree — the counter-decree permitting Jewish self-defense. Where Addition B authorized genocide, Addition E reverses it completely. The decree denounces Haman as a Macedonian traitor, praises the Jews as 'children of the Most High, the living God,' and commands all provinces to support the Jews' right to defend themselves.
preamble.remarkable: The decree is a remarkable document of Hellenistic Jewish self-representation. A Persian king confesses that the God of Israel governs the kingdom, calls the Jews children of the Most High, and denounces their persecutor as a Macedonian agent. The identification of Haman as a Macedonian rather than an Agagite/Amalekite reframes the ancient enmity in terms meaningful to a Greek-speaking audience living under Macedonian successor states.
preamble.friction: This addition has no Hebrew counterpart. Its elaborate Hellenistic rhetorical style differs markedly from the MT's simpler prose. The theological claims — a Persian king confessing Israel's God — parallel the Daniel tradition but exceed anything in the MT Esther. The 'Macedonian' identification of Haman is historically anachronistic for the Persian period.
preamble.connections: Esther 8:9-14 (MT decree summary); Addition B (first decree, now reversed); Daniel 3:28-29, 6:25-27 (pagan kings confessing Israel's God); 3 Maccabees 7 (similar reversal decree); Ezra 1:1-4 (Cyrus' decree for Jewish restoration).

verses.text_source: Greek: Τόδε ἐστὶν τὸ ἀντίγραφον τῆς ἐπιστολῆς
verses.text_reference: The following is a copy of this letter: 'The Great King, Artaxerxes, to the governors of the provinces from India to Ethiopia, one hundred twenty-seven provinces, and to those who are loyal to our government, greetings.'
verses.rendering: The following is a copy of this letter: 'The Great King Artaxerxes, to the governors of the one hundred twenty-seven provinces from India to Ethiopia, and to those loyal to our government — greetings.'
translator_notes: Addition E parallels Addition B but reverses it: where B authorized destruction, E authorizes Jewish self-defense and denounces Haman. The formal chancellery style matches B.

verses.text_source: Greek: πολλοὶ τῇ πλείστῃ τῶν εὐεργετούντων χρηστότητι
verses.text_reference: 'Many people, the more they are honored with the most generous kindness of their benefactors, the more proud do they become.'
verses.rendering: 'Many who have been showered with the generous kindness of their benefactors become all the more arrogant.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ οὐ μόνον τοὺς ὑποτεταγμένους ἡμῖν ζητοῦσιν κακοποιεῖν
verses.text_reference: 'and not only seek to injure our subjects, but in their inability to bear prosperity, they even undertake to scheme against their own benefactors.'
verses.rendering: They not only seek to harm our subjects, but, unable to bear their own prosperity, they even plot against their benefactors.'

verses.text_source: Greek: οὐ μόνον εὐχαριστίαν ἐξαιρούμενοι ἐξ ἀνθρώπων
verses.text_reference: 'They not only take away thankfulness from among humankind, but, exalted by the boasts of those who know nothing of goodness, they suppose that they will escape the evil-hating justice of God, who always sees everything.'
verses.rendering: 'They not only drive out gratitude from among mortals, but — puffed up by the praises of those who know nothing of goodness — they suppose they will escape the justice of the evil-hating God who sees all things.'
translator_notes: The decree explicitly invokes 'the evil-hating God who sees all things' — theological language entirely absent from the MT of Esther. This transforms a royal political document into a theological confession.

verses.text_source: Greek: πολλάκις δὲ καὶ πολλοὺς τῶν ἐπ᾽ ἐξουσίαις τεταγμένων
verses.text_reference: 'And often many of those who are set in places of authority have been made in part responsible for the shedding of innocent blood.'
verses.rendering: 'Often, indeed, many who are entrusted with authority have been made partly responsible for the shedding of innocent blood,'

verses.text_source: Greek: τῇ τῶν ἐμπιστευθέντων φίλων κακοηθείᾳ πεισθέντες
verses.text_reference: 'and have been involved in irremediable calamities, by the persuasion of friends who have been entrusted with the administration of public affairs.'
verses.rendering: having been persuaded by the malice of friends entrusted with public affairs, and becoming enmeshed in irremediable calamities.

verses.text_source: Greek: παραλογιζομένων τὴν ἀκέραιον τῶν ἐπικρατούντων εὐγνωμοσύνην
verses.text_reference: 'when these friends, by the false trickery of their evil natures, beguile the sincere goodwill of their sovereigns.'
verses.rendering: These so-called friends deceive the sincere goodwill of their sovereigns through the false cunning of their wicked natures.'

verses.text_source: Greek: σκοπεῖν δὲ ἔξεστιν
verses.text_reference: 'What has been wickedly accomplished through the pestilent behavior of those who exercise authority unworthily, can be seen.'
verses.rendering: 'What has been wickedly accomplished through the corrupt conduct of those who exercise authority unworthily is plain to see —

verses.text_source: Greek: οὐ τοσοῦτον ἐκ τῶν παλαιοτέρων ἱστοριῶν
verses.text_reference: 'not so much from the more ancient records we hand on, as from investigation of matters close at hand.'
verses.rendering: not so much from the ancient histories we have received, as from examining what lies right before our eyes.'

verses.text_source: Greek: ὅσα γὰρ ἠδικήθημεν τῆς Αμαν τοῦ Αμαδαθου Μακεδόνος
verses.text_reference: 'For Haman son of Hammedatha, a Macedonian — having not a drop of Persian blood, and quite alien to our kindliness — was hospitably received by us.'
verses.rendering: 'For Haman, son of Hammedatha — a Macedonian, truly alien to the Persian blood and far removed from our kindness — was received by us as a guest.
translator_notes: Haman is here called a 'Macedonian' (Makedonos), not an Agagite. This reflects Hellenistic-era hostility toward Macedonians (the culture of Alexander's successors, including the Seleucids who persecuted Jews). The LXX reframes the Esther story's villain in terms relevant to its Greek-speaking audience.

translator_notes.note: Greek Makedonos — the LXX identifies Haman not as an Amalekite (Agagite) but as a Macedonian. This reflects Hellenistic political realities and may have functioned as anti-Seleucid propaganda.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἐπιτυχὼν τῆς παρ᾽ ἡμῖν εὐνοίας
verses.text_reference: 'He so far enjoyed the goodwill that we have for every nation that he was called our father and was continually bowed down to by all as the person second to the royal throne.'
verses.rendering: He enjoyed such goodwill as we extend to every nation that he was called our father, and all continually bowed before him as the one second to the royal throne.'

verses.text_source: Greek: μὴ ἐνέγκας δὲ τὴν ὑπερηφανίαν
verses.text_reference: 'But, unable to restrain his arrogance, he undertook to deprive us of our kingdom and our life.'
verses.rendering: 'But, unable to restrain his arrogance, he conspired to deprive us of our kingdom and of our very life,

verses.text_source: Greek: τόν τε ἡμέτερον σωτῆρα καὶ διὰ παντὸς εὐεργέτην Μαρδοχαῖον
verses.text_reference: 'and with intricate craft and deceit, asked for the destruction of Mordecai, our savior and perpetual benefactor, and of Esther, the blameless partner of our kingdom, together with their whole nation.'
verses.rendering: and by intricate deception demanded the destruction of Mordecai — our savior and constant benefactor — and of Esther, the blameless partner of our kingdom, together with their whole nation.'

verses.text_source: Greek: τούτοις γὰρ τοῖς τρόποις ᾤετο
verses.text_reference: 'He thought that by these methods he would catch us undefended and would transfer the kingdom of the Persians to the Macedonians.'
verses.rendering: 'For by these means he thought to catch us without allies and to transfer the sovereignty of the Persians to the Macedonians.'
translator_notes: The decree reveals Haman's alleged true motive: to transfer Persian sovereignty to Macedonia. This geopolitical framing makes the Esther story relevant to Hellenistic power struggles.

verses.text_source: Greek: ἡμεῖς δὲ τοὺς ὑπὸ τοῦ τρισαλιτηρίου παραδεδομένους Ιουδαίους
verses.text_reference: 'But we find that the Jews, who were consigned to annihilation by this thrice-accursed man, are not evildoers, but are governed by most righteous laws.'
verses.rendering: 'But we find that the Jews — whom this thrice-accursed wretch consigned to destruction — are not evildoers, but are governed by the most righteous laws.

verses.text_source: Greek: εἰσὶν δὲ υἱοὶ τοῦ ὑψίστου μεγίστου ζῶντος θεοῦ
verses.text_reference: 'and are children of the living God, most high, most mighty, who has directed the kingdom both for us and for our ancestors in the most excellent order.'
verses.rendering: They are children of the Most High, the most mighty, the living God, who has guided this kingdom for us and for our fathers in the most excellent order.'
translator_notes: 'Children of the living God, Most High' — the Persian king confesses Israelite theology. This mirrors the confessions of Nebuchadnezzar in Daniel 2-4 and Darius in Daniel 6. The pattern of pagan rulers acknowledging Israel's God is a major theme in diaspora literature.

verses.text_source: Greek: καλῶς οὖν ποιήσετε μὴ χρησάμενοι τοῖς ὑπὸ Αμαν
verses.text_reference: 'You will therefore do well not to put in execution the letters sent by Haman son of Hammedatha.'
verses.rendering: 'You will therefore do well not to act on the letters sent by Haman, son of Hammedatha,

verses.text_source: Greek: ὅτι αὐτὸς ὁ ταῦτα κατεργασάμενος πρὸς ταῖς Σουσικαῖς πύλαις ἐσταύρωται
verses.text_reference: 'since he, the one who devised these things, has been hanged at the gate of Susa — with all his household.'
verses.rendering: since the one who devised these things has been impaled at the gates of Susa — together with all his household.'
translator_notes: The LXX uses estaurōtai (has been crucified/impaled) — the same verb later used for the crucifixion of Jesus. In the Persian context, this refers to impalement on a stake.

verses.text_source: Greek: τοῦ πάντων ἐπικρατοῦντος θεοῦ κατ᾽ ἀξίαν αὐτῷ κρίσιν ἐπιθέντος
verses.text_reference: 'For the God who rules over all things has speedily returned his deeds upon his own head, in the judgment he deserved.'
verses.rendering: 'For the God who governs all things has swiftly brought upon him the judgment he deserved.'

verses.text_source: Greek: ἐκθέντες οὖν τὸ ἀντίγραφον τῆσδε τῆς ἐπιστολῆς ἐν παντὶ τόπῳ
verses.text_reference: 'Therefore post a copy of this letter publicly in every place, and permit the Jews to live under their own laws.'
verses.rendering: 'Therefore post a copy of this letter in every public place, and allow the Jews to observe their own laws.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ συνεπισχύειν αὐτοῖς
verses.text_reference: 'And give them reinforcements, so that on the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, Adar, they may defend themselves against those who attack them on the day of oppression.'
verses.rendering: And give them support, so that on the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, Adar, they may defend themselves against any who attack them on their day of affliction.'

verses.text_source: Greek: ὅτι ταύτην ὁ πάντα ἐπικρατῶν θεός
verses.text_reference: 'For God, who rules over all things, has made this day to be a joy for his chosen people instead of a day of destruction for them.'
verses.rendering: 'For the God who governs all things has turned this day from a day of destruction into a day of gladness for his chosen people.'

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ὑμεῖς οὖν ἐν ταῖς ἐπωνύμοις ὑμῶν ἑορταῖς
verses.text_reference: 'Therefore you shall observe this with all good cheer as a notable day among your commemorative festivals.'
verses.rendering: 'You shall therefore celebrate it with all rejoicing as a notable day among your commemorative festivals,

verses.text_source: Greek: ὅπως καὶ νῦν καὶ μετὰ ταῦτα σωτηρία ᾖ ἡμῖν
verses.text_reference: 'so that both now and hereafter it may represent deliverance for us and the loyal Persians, but for those who plot against us it may be a reminder of destruction.'
verses.rendering: so that both now and hereafter it may stand as a memorial of deliverance for us and for loyal Persians, but for those who conspire against us, a reminder of their destruction.'
translator_notes: The decree concludes by establishing Purim as a joint Jewish-Persian holiday — a remarkable claim of shared civic identity between the Jewish community and the imperial government.
